The problem with executive communications is that we forget about creating a quality communication, something that is clear, something that is compelling, something that is consistent. Tell me about it. Five minutes later, she was still talking. She failed the dinner party test because you don't pay attention to someone for five minutes. The cost here is an opportunity cost. We all have these communications opportunities every day. It could be you're sitting on the plane next to a guy like Jay. The opportunity cost is that you lose their attention and you lose their trust pretty quickly because you can't help selling because you haven't thought through your communications.

## [09:09 - 12:12]

That brings us to what I call the story platform. Magic it shows up. Awesome. The story platform is a little framework I put together to codify the essential elements of a company. Now, it could also apply to a team. So you're working for a team within a company or it could apply to a person as a leader. You can have your own story platform as a leader or you could have all three. What does it do? It is the foundation for all strategic communications. The tech world, we think of platforms. Windows is a platform. iOS is a platform. There's many platforms. What you want to do is create a platform and then a platform is something upon which many other solutions and applications can be built. So think of the story platform as a platform for any sort of executive communications. It could be speeches, it could be blog posts. It could be how you describe your company, your elevator pitch. It's your platform for all of these communications. One of the great things that it does is it aligns the team. Remember, one of the aspects of communications, of quality communications is consistency and not just consistency from today to tomorrow when I describe things, but consistency from when you and your team members, your colleagues, your stakeholders, when they describe things. I have a client who's a technology startup in Silicon Valley and when I was first brought in, they didn't have a sales pitch. I was brought in to create a sales pitch. ## [12:12 - 15:13]

Respect each other, respect opportunity. Each one of those values had like five subvalues and then there was a description around each of those. No one in the company knew. They were inauthentic. How many of you read really bad vision statements? Yeah, like go on any Fortune 500 company. Our vision is to be the number one provider of this and generating significant customer happiness and raising shareholder value. Is that exciting? What's the purpose of a vision statement? I would argue the purpose of a vision statement is to inspire. You want people to be inspired by what you're trying to achieve. So Tesla's vision statement is something like accelerate humanities transition to sustainable energy. You know, not like we're going to be the number one car provider or we're going to sell really sexy electric cars or whatever. Now, that's kind of a cool vision. So my definition of a vision is what does great look like? And not great from your company perspective or your shareholder perspective. What does great look like from the world's perspective? When each of your companies, when you have knocked it out of the park, when you've got a grand slam to use a baseball metaphor, how is the world a better place? How is the world different or changed? And it could be grandiose like accelerator transformation to sustainable energy or maybe a little more micro. Everybody in a particular industry is so much more efficient and better at their job that they all go home early and can have dinner with their family. I don't know. But it's something to inspire. A vision is to inspire. So what can you say about yourself as a leader or a company that would be inspirational to other people? It's not about you. It's not about your company. It's about how things are different when you've done your very best, when you've reached your potential. Okay, so that's vision. All right, Brian will go insights. The problem is, I mentioned this earlier, we're all told, okay, to be great leaders, be great storytellers.

interesting insights? And like I'm working with a client now and the CEO, they're a, they're a technology company in the artificial intelligence space. They're based in Silicon Valley. But as most of you know, that's not when the interesting stuff happens. The interesting stuff happens when you walk into the room and, oh, hey, there's your, your client or your friend you haven't seen in a while. Or when you, when you, when you close the presentation and people start filtering out. You know, so those are the conversations. And if you want to get at your interesting insights, just start talking to people. Because as soon as you start writing or putting together a PowerPoint, your mind shifts and you, you get boring. So I guess my point is keep it conversational. And, and I think a lot of people are afraid to be natural and vulnerable. Alan, how do you address that? Well, that gets back into, you know, more of the aspects of modern leadership. Well, it is about communications. But in our, I co-authored a book called Trillion Dollar Coach, which is about an executive coach in Silicon Valley. His name was Bill Campbell. And he had a few different, I created what I called the leadership formula. Bill, Bill's, the trillion dollar coach is leadership formula. Leadership is management plus coaching times love. So management is what a lot of people are very good at and really think about operations, running a good business, hitting your numbers. Coaching is where the vulnerability comes in. Because coaching is all about helping the people that you work with and especially the people that work for you be as successful as possible in their own, from their own perspective, whatever success means to them. And so that involves creating trust and really listening to them and understanding them.

## [39:28 - 42:33]

What do you want out of your career? God, what excites you? You know, that's sort of career development, coaching conversation. And usually there's kind of a quick pro-qual involved. Like you got to be open with them as well. And it doesn't mean you got to go like, you know, talk to them about your deep depression or your anxieties or, you know, what goes on in life. But just be real. I mean, I think we've all worked for leaders who like to be a leader. I think we've all worked for leaders who like, sometimes you can sense they're just not real. They're feeding you a line. And so I try to break through that by just like, how would you speak to your partner or your spouse? You'd be real with them, I hope. And that is a important part of coaching is being real with people. And I think we're also in an environment where it's really almost required of us as leaders. You know, a generation ago, when I got out of college and in business school, it was about the title and the compensation and, you know, the role and the career path. And now it's a lot more about purpose, the teamwork, the psychological safety. This is a generational shift in what's required and expected in a workplace culture. But it is required now for success. Interesting. How does somebody who's not as acclimated or, or predisposed, embrace it, you know, transition themselves and, and really enjoy the process? Yeah, that's a good question, Jake, since I'm, I'm not inclined that way. We tell an anecdote in our book, $20 Coach Battle Gentlemen, and I'm blanking on his name. I'll come up with an omitted. Who is the CEO of Adobe Systems? And he was a salesperson in Adobe. He had always been a technical leadership. So it was the first non-technology leadership. And so what he forced himself to do was just go talk to the engineers, like go sit down with them at lunch, talk to them in the elevators, make the time to just walk around and chat with people. And, you know, rather than in a formal communication environment, in a formal meeting environment, take the time to break those barriers down. And that's really effective. And most people, like especially most leaders, like all of you in the room, you're extremely personable one-on-one. Like it's not that hard. It just takes, you have to make the time to do it. And again, being vulnerable and being authentic, again, doesn't mean you have to expose all your secrets or, you know, your true deep

## [42:33 - 45:33]

vulnerabilities. Just needs being real with people. Talk to them about your family, about their family. Just take the time to do that. I think it's, it can be highly effective. Now, you know, maybe not everybody can do it. We had a situation once where my friend, Jonathan and I, he was my boss and he was the head of the company. He was the head of all products at Google. You have like a thousand people who are product managers. And Bill Campbell was an executive coach at Google. He was also the executive coach to Steve Jobs and people at Apple. He was the executive chairman into it. And he really was quite an amazing guy in Silicon Valley. And there was a senior leader on the product team who was really struggling with this team. And Jonathan and I said, we asked, hey, Bill, can you go talk to this guy? See what's going on. And Bill comes back, you know, a couple of weeks later, he's like, you know, what the F did you want me to talk to that guy for? Well, we wanted, we wanted him to, you know, he needs to have a stronger bond with his team. Like he needs to show that he cares about him. And Bill goes, you know, to care about people, you have to actually care about people. So, you know, every once in a while, there's people that just don't care about people. But I expect most leaders really aren't like that. So just take the time to. I don't know, it sounds trite, but just talk to people. And, you know, I think, you know, we're getting back earlier, Jay, we were talking about communications. And again, I see communications as such a powerful tool. I got this question a lot when I was giving presentations and talking to clients when I was at Google, which is, OK, what can they do? What can they change? Well, as a leader in a large organization, there's a couple of things you can change. You can change how you communicate and you can change who you hire and how you hire. Those are the levers you have that you really do have control over. And so, like I said, I ran internal communication for a while for my boss. And, you know, it's it's just so important how you communicate, how you just tell that story. What's your personal story? What's your leadership story? You know, why did if you started the company, why? Every single company was a cool startup at one point. What's that story? Why did you do it?
